title: An Everything-but-Movies Mailbag
format: With Amanda about to go on leave, Sean pitched an episode that never mentions movies. Everyone broke the rule anyway. A banked mailbag (producer Bobby Wagner reads the questions) on the NL MVP race, the Sean-and-Amanda origin story, email etiquette, death-row meals, toddler logic, cocktails, and whether you'd fight one horse-sized duck or a hundred duck-sized horses. The films slip in anyway.

segments.heading: The Mailbag
segments.note: Sean and Amanda, with Bobby Wagner reading listener questions

films.title: The Avengers
films.note: The literal origin story of the show: Sean and Amanda both wound up at a press screening of it, then a Brooklyn bar afterward. Sean calls it the closest thing the podcast has to a founding myth.

films.title: What's Your Number?
films.note: The Anna Faris comedy (also with Chris Evans) whose press screening is where Amanda and her now-husband Zach Baron officially met.

films.title: Anora
films.note: Cited as proof the new stuff is harder to come by than the old: they'd just done an episode on it and Sean loved having a recent movie he was genuinely jazzed about.

films.title: Nickel Boys
films.note: One of the fall titles Sean points to as feeling unlike anything he'd seen, after a summer where every movie was a three-star.

films.title: Alien: Romulus
films.note: Brought up on the Industry question: David Jonsson was one of the best parts of it, evidence he can carry a bigger film career.

films.title: Back to Black
films.note: Marisa Abela's Amy Winehouse biopic, which Sean says didn't work out (though not through any fault of hers) while arguing she looks like a movie star.

films.title: Conclave
films.note: Releasing the day this episode airs. Amanda can't wait to see it as a civilian: a highbrow-dumb movie, fancy actors in fancy clothes, lifted from an airport novel.

films.title: The Substance
films.note: Surfaces during a retinol tangent. Amanda says it speaks to ideas Sean's interested in; she's only unsure about the execution, and can't believe he still hasn't seen it.

films.title: Devil
films.note: Sean's reference for the stuck-in-an-elevator question: the M. Night Shyamalan-produced movie about eight people trapped in an elevator, one of whom is the devil.

films.title: 13 Hours: The Secret Soldiers of Benghazi
films.note: Sean's evidence for picking Michael Bay to make a January 6th movie: not Bay's best, and you can feel him straining to have something to say about a thing there's nothing to say about.

films.title: Sully
films.note: Named as the case where Clint Eastwood's 'I understand this better than everyone' instinct lands. Sean would rather watch prime Clint's version of the January 6th movie.

films.title: Richard Jewell
films.note: Cited as a brave act and proof Eastwood cares about journalism, even when Sean doesn't love the man's politics.

films.title: Juror #2
films.note: Eastwood's new one, a week out at the time. Sean nearly calls it 'the rural juror'; both figure it could be good.

films.title: Ambulance
films.note: The Bay movie Sean adores. He goes on a tear defending Bay against a Hollywood that abandoned him, demanding they hand him G.I. Joe and the Ninja Turtles on top of Transformers.

films.title: Father of the Bride
films.note: The last movie that made Amanda cry: she rewatched the Steve Martin version while pregnant during the Steve Martin episode and things, she says, got out of hand.

films.title: Armageddon
films.note: The film that made Amanda cry the most, factually: she saw it at 15 and didn't recover for several days.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: Sean's childhood gut-punch (they did Mufasa like that?). Meanwhile his daughter is Team Scar.

films.title: Horizon: An American Saga - Chapter 1
films.note: Bobby says he sobbed through the whole final act, undone as much by the sheer incomprehensibility of it as anything. Nobody's sure what happened to the Venice screening of Chapter 2.

films.title: Sing Sing
films.note: One of the few 2024 releases that got Sean: maybe not crying, but emotionally affected.

films.title: You Should Have Left
films.note: Comes up on the bad-interactions question: the David Koepp horror movie with Kevin Bacon and Amanda Seyfried, released during COVID. Koepp came on the show and, Sean says, was needlessly prickly, so he won't be invited back, though Sean still expects to like Koepp's work.

films.title: Coco
films.note: The film that made Bobby cry the most, no contest. He didn't see it until he was an adult working at The Ringer; it got him good.

films: G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra

Podcasts: The New York Times Book Review podcast
Books: Death at the Sign of the Rook
